title: Wooden Gear Wall Clock
summary: A wall clock shaped like a mechanical gear, cut from a single board with a scroll saw and finished with a standard quartz movement.
description:
This clock gets its entire visual identity from one cut — the gear-tooth silhouette — rather than from an elaborate mechanism. A standard off-the-shelf quartz clock movement does all the actual timekeeping; the woodworking is entirely in the shape.

The **board needs to be at least 1/4" thick to accept the clock mechanism's shaft**, which is the one hard constraint on material choice. Beyond that, any board wide enough for a roughly 12" circle works.

A printed gear template, taped or spray-adhesived to the board, replaces a numeric cut list entirely here — trace the template rather than trying to reconstruct gear-tooth geometry from measurements.

tools.note: Or a scroll saw for tighter tooth detail.
Drills the center hole sized to the clock mechanism's shaft.

materials.name: Board, at least 1/4" thick, cut to 12in

steps.title: Cut the blank
steps.body: Cut the clock blank 12" long from the board. A 2x12's actual width is 11 1/4", so the blank comes out 11 1/4" x 12" and the largest true circle you can get from it is 11 1/4" across, not the 12" the summary suggests. Cut both ends square — a printed template is going onto this face, and a skewed blank wastes template. Check your movement's shaft against the blank's thickness before you go any further: a 2x12 is 1 1/2" thick, and the shaft has to pass through and still leave thread for the nut on the front.
materials: Board, at least 1/4" thick, cut to 12in

steps.title: Transfer the gear template
steps.body: Print the gear template sized to fit inside the blank — no more than 11 1/4" across, since that is the board's real width. Find and mark the exact center of the blank first, then line the template's center up to that mark so the hands end up centered in the gear. Tape it down all the way around, or use spray adhesive; a template that lifts halfway through a tooth is how a tooth goes wrong. Trace the outline in pencil as well, so you still have a line to follow if the paper tears mid-cut.
materials: Board, at least 1/4" thick, cut to 12in

steps.title: Cut the gear shape
steps.body: Cut the silhouette out with the jigsaw, staying just outside the line and cleaning back to it afterward rather than trying to split the line in one pass. Take each tooth notch as two straight cuts into the corner instead of turning the blade inside the notch — a jigsaw blade twisted in a tight radius wanders and bevels the edge. Drill a relief hole at each inside corner if the notches are tight, and let the blade reach full speed before it touches the wood. Support the offcut as you finish each cut so it does not break away and tear the last tooth off with it.
materials: Board, at least 1/4" thick, cut to 12in

steps.title: Drill the center hole
steps.body: Drill the center hole at the mark you made, sized to your movement's shaft — hold the shaft against the forstner bit to match it rather than guessing a diameter. Back the blank with a scrap so the bit does not blow the face out as it exits. If the blank is thicker than the shaft is long, counterbore a recess on the BACK face with a larger forstner bit until the remaining thickness lets the shaft through with thread to spare; a 2x12 blank is 1 1/2" thick, which is more than most quartz shafts will reach through. Test-fit the movement before you finish anything.
materials: Board, at least 1/4" thick, cut to 12in

steps.title: Sand and finish
steps.body: Sand both faces and every cut edge, working carefully into the tooth notches where the jigsaw left marks — those inside corners are what the eye actually reads as a gear. Ease the sharp corners so the finish does not rub thin on them later. Paint or stain to taste and let it cure fully before the movement goes in. Keep finish out of the center hole and off the back recess, so the movement still seats flat against the wood.

steps.title: Install the mechanism
steps.body: Push the shaft through the center hole from the back, fit the washer and nut on the front, and snug it just enough to hold — over-tightening a quartz movement's plastic case will crack it. Press on the hour hand, then the minute hand, then the second hand, checking that none of them touch each other or the face as they sweep past twelve. Set the time with the movement's own knob rather than by dragging the hands around. Fit the battery and hang it from the movement's built-in hanger.
